notes.anchor: for a good man, to be at Rome is the most wretched lot of all
notes.note: Torquatus was in exile or self-imposed absence abroad after Pharsalus (48 BC), while his property and family remained in Rome; the paradox Cicero exploits is that Rome itself, under Caesar's post-Pharsalus settlement, is the worse place to be.

notes.anchor: the man who up to now has been more unjust to you than your dignity called for
notes.note: Caesar, unnamed but unmistakable: he had not yet granted Torquatus's petition for restoration, though Cicero detects signs that Caesar's hostility was softening. The circumlocution is characteristic of the dangerous candour of these private consolation letters.

notes.anchor: by his consciousness of the best counsels
notes.note: A central Stoic-inflected consolation: the wise man's inner moral self-approval (conscientia) is independent of external fortune; Cicero draws on this tradition throughout Fam. 6.1–6.4 as the only consolation that cannot be stripped away by defeat.

notes.anchor: virtue itself can still sustain itself
notes.note: The Stoic thesis that virtue (virtus) is the sole and sufficient good, here deployed consolatorily: even total political ruin leaves the morally upright man internally undefeated. Cicero distances himself from full Stoic rigorism elsewhere but leans on it in these civil-war letters.

notes.anchor: public right was being argued by javelins and swords, not by our policies and authority
notes.note: Cicero's recurring self-defence: he had seen from the start that the Pompeian cause was militarily weaker, and had urged a negotiated settlement rather than battle; Pharsalus vindicated his pessimism. The phrase encapsulates his anguish that the res publica was being decided by force rather than law and senatorial deliberation.
A slave or freedman of Torquatus who had visited Cicero and reported on his master's state of mind; the name is Greek ('fond of silver'), typical of servile nomenclature. He is otherwise unattested.

notes.anchor: you have Servius Sulpicius, whom you have always uniquely loved
notes.note: Ser. Sulpicius Rufus (cos. 51 BC), the leading jurist of the late Republic and Cicero's close friend; he was himself in Athens or the Greek east at this period, and Cicero sees his proximity to Torquatus as a practical substitute for his own consoling letters.
